steps.prompt: What does 'Quod si' mean as a phrase?

hints.because if: 'Quod' can mean 'because,' but 'quod si' is a fixed phrase meaning 'but if'
hints.which if: 'Quod' here is not a relative pronoun — 'quod si' is a transitional formula
hints.that if: 'Quod' is not introducing a noun clause — 'quod si' is a standard Ciceronian transition

hints.nominative: Nominative feminine would be 'ea' — 'eam' with -am is accusative
hints.ablative: Ablative feminine would be 'ea' — 'eam' is accusative
hints.genitive: Genitive feminine would be 'eius' — 'eam' is accusative

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'Catilina' — 'Catilinae' with -ae is genitive or dative
hints.dative: 'Catilinae' could be dative, but here it possesses 'audaciam' — genitive of possession

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'Catilinae' ends in -ae, marking 1st declension
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'Catilinae' ends in -ae
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'Catilinae' ends in -ae

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'audacia' — 'audaciam' with -am is accusative

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'audaciae' ends in -ae
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'audaciae' ends in -ae
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'audaciae' ends in -ae

steps.reveal: scelus = the crime (nom., subject of 'patefactum est')

hints.accusative: 'Scelus' as a neuter noun has identical nom. and acc. — here it is subject, so nominative

steps.prompt: The infinitive of 'patefactum est' translates to?

hints.to make / to do: 'To make' is 'facere' — 'patefacere' is a compound meaning to lay open or reveal
hints.to satisfy: 'To satisfy' is 'satisfacere' — 'patefacere' means to lay open
hints.to be evident: 'To be evident' is 'patere' — 'patefacere' (pate- + facere) means to make open/reveal

steps.prompt: What tense and mood is 'patefactum est'?
steps.reveal: patefactum est = has been revealed / was laid open

hints.present passive indicative: Present passive would be 'patefit' — participle + 'est' is perfect passive
hints.pluperfect passive indicative: Pluperfect passive would be 'patefactum erat' — 'est' makes it perfect
hints.perfect active indicative: Perfect active would be 'patefecit' — participle + 'est' is passive

hints.facio, facere, feci, factum: These are the parts of 'facere' (to make) — the base verb, but 'patefacere' is the compound
hints.pateo, patere, patui, —: These are the parts of 'patere' (to lie open) — intransitive, different from 'patefacere'
hints.satisfacio, satisfacere, satisfeci, sati: These are the parts of 'satisfacere' (to satisfy) — different compound

hints.it is a supine: The supine looks identical, but in this periphrastic construction it is a nominative neuter participle
hints.it is an accusative singular: While -um can mark accusative, here the participle is nominative neuter agreeing with subject 'scelus'
hints.it is an adverb: Participles are verbal adjectives, not adverbs — it agrees with 'scelus'

translation: But if that audacity of Catiline — the crime has been revealed
